region_of_origin: South and Southeast Asia; ubiquitous at Singapore hawker centres
food_subgroup: Fresh plant juice — sugarcane, pressed at point of sale, no added sugar, commonly served with ginger and lemon over ice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for fresh sugarcane juice. Closest parent concept: sugarcane product / plant-derived juice. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sugarcane juice, fresh — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0K1B is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for fresh sugarcane juice. No exact terminal code confirmed; closest_parent declared.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code confirmed for fresh sugarcane juice as a food item. Null declared to avoid fabrication.

serving.description: 1 glass (300 ml) — standard Singapore hawker centre serve with ice, ginger, and lemon
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre glass of freshly pressed sugarcane juice is approximately 300 ml (300 g), typically served over ice with a squeeze of lemon or lime and a small amount of ginger.

nutrient_source.title: Sugarcane Juice — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods + FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values drawn from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sugarcane juice and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian sugarcane juice. The 300 ml serving size reflects standard Singapore hawker-centre practice. Minor additions of ginger and lemon contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical quantities and are not separately itemised.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sugarcane Juice — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (GI range 43–55 reported across studies)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed GI literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 50 is the midpoint of the peer-reviewed range of 43–55 reported for fresh sugarcane juice in the University of Sydney GI Database and associated literature. The sucrose matrix and naturally occurring mineral and fibre fraction of the cane moderate the glycaemic response relative to refined sucrose (GI ~65). GL per 300 ml serving calculated as: (GI 50 × available carbohydrate 42 g per 300 ml) / 100 = 21.0 — classified as very high GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium but high sugar load drives long-term metabolic risk worsening hypertension
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is negligible (~12 mg per 300 ml) and potassium (~420 mg per 300 ml) is beneficial for blood pressure; however, habitual daily consumption of 42 g free sugar per glass promotes visceral adiposity and insulin resistance, which are independent drivers of hypertension — limit to occasional consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic load (GL 21 per 300 ml) from 42 g intrinsic free sugar per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: One 300 ml glass delivers 42 g of free sugar (WHO-classified) and a glycaemic load of 21 — nearly double the WHO daily free-sugar limit of 25 g — making regular consumption strongly contraindicated for people with Type 2 Diabetes; the 'natural' and 'no added sugar' framing does not reduce glycaemic impact, and patients should be counselled to eliminate or strictly limit intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar load promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation at habitual intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat and sodium are negligible, and potassium content is cardioprotective; however, 42 g of free sugar per 300 ml serving at habitual daily intake raises triglycerides, promotes hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and contributes to endothelial dysfunction — people with established cardiovascular disease should restrict intake to occasional consumption only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar load drives AGE formation and NF-κB-mediated systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: 42 g of sugar per 300 ml glass promotes advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation and NF-κB activation, directly worsening the inflammatory burden in rheumatoid arthritis — regular consumption is strongly discouraged and should be replaced with unsweetened beverages such as plain water or green tea.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sugarcane Juice (Fresh, 300 ml) — known in Chinese as 甘蔗汁 (Gānzhè zhī) or 蔗汁 (Zhèzhī), in Malay as Air Tebu, in Tamil as கரும்புச் சாறு, and in Hindi as गन्ने का रस — is a refreshing, naturally sweet beverage made by pressing peeled sugarcane stalks through a motorised or hand-cranked roller mill. The juice is a vibrant greenish-yellow colour with a clean, grassy sweetness. It is ubiquitous at Singapore hawker centres and roadside stalls, typically served over ice with a squeeze of fresh lemon or lime and a small amount of ginger to balance the intense sweetness. Per 100 g: 58 kcal, 0.1 g protein, 0.0 g fat (naturally zero — sugarcane juice is a plant-derived aqueous extract with no lipid fraction), 14.2 g carbohydrate (13.8 g sugar), 4 mg sodium, 140 mg potassium, 12 mg calcium, 0.6 mg iron. Per standard 300 ml hawker serve: approximately 174 kcal, 42 g sugar, GL 21 (very high). CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: Despite being labelled 'natural' and 'no added sugar', the intrinsic sucrose in sugarcane juice is classified as FREE SUGAR by the WHO and is fully glycaemically impactful. One 300 ml glass nearly doubles the WHO recommended daily free-sugar limit of 25 g. GI ~50 (low-moderate range per University of Sydney GI Database literature), but the very high serving-level GL of 21 makes this beverage clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes and rheumatoid arthritis management. Source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy + FAO/INFOODS SEA. Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: sugarcane juice good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are drawn from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian sugarcane juice. The 300 ml serving size reflects documented Singapore hawker-centre practice. GI is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database literature range of 43–55 for fresh sugarcane juice. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a fresh-pressed sugarcane beverage.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero — sugarcane juice is a plant-derived aqueous extract with no lipid fraction; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero in the pressed juice (fibre remains in the bagasse after pressing); confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero — plant-derived beverage. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, and folate_mcg_dfe are reported as 0.0 reflecting below-detection or negligible trace levels in approved sources; these are confirmed zero or trace per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The GI of 50 represents the midpoint of the peer-reviewed range (43–55); GL of 21 per 300 ml serving is very high and clinically significant. Minor additions of ginger and lemon at typical hawker quantities contribute negligible macronutrient change. Duplicate portions in the input (six identical 250 ml entries) have been consolidated to one secondary portion entry. FoodOn URI is null — no exact FoodOn code confirmed for fresh sugarcane juice; closest_parent declared to avoid fabrication. SNOMED CT code is null — no confirmed food-item code; null declared to avoid fabrication.
